food_subgroup: Prawn / shrimp curry dish with vegetables and spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific term for Jhinga Jalfrezi; 'shrimp dish' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class. Exact match not available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked prawn preparation with vegetables and spices.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Jalfrezi-style prawn dishes. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Jhinga Jalfrezi or analogous North Indian prawn dish. Leaving null to maintain ontology honesty.

preparation_method: spiced with whole and ground spices

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Jhinga Jalfrezi is approximately 300 g, comprising prawns, mixed vegetables, and sauce, served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn curry / cooked prawn with spices and vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Jhinga Jalfrezi exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn curry data (prawns cooked with oil, onion, tomato, spices) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and mixed vegetable contribution. Macros reflect a composite of approximately 55% prawn, 30% vegetables (bell pepper, onion, tomato), and 15% oil-spice-sauce matrix. Energy calculated using Atwater factors. This is an estimated composition; exact restaurant formulation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — seafood and mixed protein-vegetable dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jhinga Jalfrezi. GI is estimated at 15 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked seafood and mixed vegetable dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Cooked shrimp and prawn dishes with vegetables and spices have negligible starch content; GI is expected to be very low (≤20). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 1.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a 300 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150–200 g, as the sodium load from a full restaurant serving can be clinically significant for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8 g per 100 g) and high protein (13.5 g per 100 g) result in a glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 1.7 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jhinga Jalfrezi is a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic load and high protein content, which supports satiety and minimises postprandial blood glucose excursions; sodium monitoring remains important.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Prawns contain cholesterol (approximately 130 mg per 100 g) but are low in saturated fat (0.9 g per 100 g); the dish provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and is relatively low in total fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Jhinga Jalfrezi can be consumed in moderate portions as part of a heart-healthy diet given its low saturated fat profile, but the cholesterol content and high sodium load warrant attention; preparation with minimal oil is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jhinga Jalfrezi is a North Indian spiced prawn dish in which whole or halved prawns (shrimp) are stir-fried with bell peppers, onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, green chillies, and a blend of dry spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. The cooking technique — jalfrezi — involves high-heat sautéing in oil, producing a semi-dry, intensely flavoured dish with a characteristic smoky, spicy, and tangy profile. The texture contrasts tender, succulent prawns against slightly crisp vegetables. Originating in the Indian subcontinent, jalfrezi-style cooking became popular in British-Indian restaurant cuisine and is now widely served across North Indian restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it appears on menus at establishments such as Bombay Curry And Kebabs, Aryana, Khushboo, and Curry Village. Nutritionally, Jhinga Jalfrezi is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 112 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Its very low glycemic index (estimated ~15) and glycemic load (~1.7 per 300 g serving) make it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content, which can be elevated in restaurant preparations. Prawns are a source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and cholesterol, and the vegetable component contributes vitamin C, vitamin A, and dietary fibre.
common_user_queries: Is jhinga jalfrezi good for diabetics?
Can I eat shrimp jalfrezi with high blood pressure?
meal_context: served with naan, roti, or basmati rice
part of a shared Indian spread

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Jhinga Jalfrezi exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn curry data and USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp values, with a composite estimate for the vegetable and sauce matrix. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn curry and USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) as no exact Jhinga Jalfrezi entry exists in any approved primary source. Actual restaurant values will vary by recipe, oil quantity, and portion size. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source data for this dish type. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed negligible/trace for a home-style Indian prawn preparation without hydrogenated fats, consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA data. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Jhinga Jalfrezi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. Sodium value of 420 mg per 100 g reflects a typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home preparations may be lower. 6. The dish is served across multiple North Indian restaurants in Singapore (field survey data: Bombay Curry And Kebabs, Aryana, Khushboo, Curry Village) at SGD 19.90–24.90 per portion. 7. FoodOn match FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are closest_parent-level matches only; no exact ontology code exists for this dish.
